---
title: "Madagascar Influenza — Phylogenetic Analysis"
subtitle: "IQ-TREE ML trees · Monophyletic clade analysis"
author: "Camila Duitama"
date: today
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    output-file: "trees.html"
    output-dir: "../docs"
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

RESULTS_DIR = Path("../results")
TREES_DIR   = Path("../trees")
DATA_DIR    = Path("../data")

summary = pd.read_csv(RESULTS_DIR / "clade_summary.tsv", sep="\t")
details = pd.read_csv(RESULTS_DIR / "clade_details.tsv", sep="\t")
metadata = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "combined_metadata.tsv", sep="\t", low_memory=False)

# Subtype → metadata Subtype label
SUBTYPE_MAP = {
    "H3N2": "A / H3N2",
    "H1N1": "A / H1N1",
    "B":    "B",
    "H9N2": "A / H9N2",
    "H6N2": "A / H6N2",
}

def parse_tree_name(name):
    parts = name.split("_", 1)
    return parts[0], parts[1]  # seg, subtype
```

← [Back to main report](index.html)

---

## Pipeline

**Question:** do Madagascar sequences form monophyletic clades (local transmission) or are they singletons (sporadic importation)?

Up to **16 maximum-likelihood trees** are built across all segment × subtype combinations with ≥ 10 Madagascar and ≥ 20 other African sequences.
All sequences are used — **no subsampling**.

| Category | Segments | Strategy | Trees |
|----------|----------|----------|-------|
| HA per-subtype | HA | One tree per HA subtype | up to 5 |
| NA per-subtype | NA | One tree per NA subtype | up to 5 |
| Internal merged | PB2, PB1, PA, NP, MP, NS | All subtypes merged per segment | 6 |
| **Total** | | | **up to 16** |

**Tree naming:** each tree is named `<segment>_<subtype>`. For example, `HA_B` means the **Hemagglutinin (HA) gene segment** of **Influenza B** viruses; `NA_H3N2` means the **Neuraminidase (NA) gene segment** of **H3N2** viruses. HA and NA are the two surface proteins that determine subtype (the H and N numbers) and are the main targets of the immune response.

### IQ-TREE parameters

Each tree was built with **IQ-TREE 3.1.0** using the following command:

```bash
iqtree3 -s <alignment.fasta> \
        -m HKY+G          \ # substitution model: HKY (transitions ≠ transversions) + Gamma rate variation
        -B 1000           \ # ultrafast bootstrap (UFBoot2): 1000 replicates, values shown as node labels
        -T <N_CPUs>       \ # multi-threading
        --seed 42         \ # reproducibility
        --prefix <NAME>   \ # output prefix
        --redo               # overwrite existing run
```

| Parameter | Value | Meaning |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| `-m HKY+G` | HKY + Gamma | 2-parameter nucleotide model (κ for ts/tv ratio) with 4 discrete rate categories |
| `-B 1000` | UFBoot2 | Ultrafast bootstrap approximation; node support values are bootstrap percentages |
| `--seed 42` | 42 | Fixed random seed for reproducibility |
| No `-bb` / no `-alrt` | — | Only UFBoot2 support; no SH-aLRT |

**Branch support:** node labels are UFBoot2 percentages (0–100). Values ≥ 95 are considered strongly supported.

**No subsampling:** all Africa sequences (Madagascar + rest of Africa) with the relevant segment enter the alignment directly. IQ-TREE internally deduplicates identical sequences for the ML optimisation, but adds them back as tips in the final tree — so **every input sequence appears in the treefile**.

### Clade detection

Before looking for Madagascar-only groups, every tree branch with UFBoot2 support **< 70** is collapsed into a polytomy (a node with more than two children, representing uncertainty). This is because UFBoot2 values — like all bootstrap values — are a measure of confidence: a value of 70 means that in 70% of bootstrap replicates the same branching pattern was recovered. Keeping branches below this threshold would mean counting groupings that are essentially random noise. Collapsing them is a conservative choice that avoids artificially inflating the number of clades.

A **monophyletic Madagascar clade** is then defined as the largest group of tree tips (≥ 2) that are all exclusively from Madagascar and share a common ancestor not shared with any non-Madagascar sequence.

## Statistics computed per tree

| Metric | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| `n_mdg_clades` | Number of monophyletic Madagascar clades (≥ 2 tips) |
| `mdg_pd_fraction` | What fraction of the tree's total branch length is "owned" by Madagascar sequences (see below) |
| `mrca_subtree_fraction` | How much of the tree sits inside the subtree that spans all Madagascar sequences (see below) |

**`mdg_pd_fraction` — how much of the tree's evolutionary space do Madagascar sequences cover?**

Imagine the tree as a road map: every branch is a road segment with a length equal to the number of mutations along that branch. Faith's Phylogenetic Diversity (PD) for Madagascar is the total length of all roads you need to drive to visit every Madagascar sequence starting from the root. If Madagascar sequences are scattered all over the tree, you need to drive almost the entire map — high PD fraction. If they all cluster in one corner, you only need a short detour — low PD fraction. This metric is divided by the total map length so it becomes a percentage of the whole tree.

**`mrca_subtree_fraction` — how spread out are Madagascar sequences across the tree?**

The **MRCA (Most Recent Common Ancestor)** of all Madagascar sequences is the single node in the tree that is the common ancestor of every Madagascar tip. It is essentially the point where all Madagascar lineages diverge. The subtree *below* that node contains everything nested between Madagascar sequences — including non-Madagascar sequences that happen to sit among them. `mrca_subtree_fraction` is the branch-length sum of that whole subtree divided by the total tree length.

- **Value ≈ 1.0** (e.g., Flu B, H3N2): The MRCA is near the root, meaning Madagascar sequences span almost the entire tree. This happens when Madagascar has sequences in many different global lineages — consistent with frequent re-importation every season.
- **Value ≈ 0.03** (e.g., NA_H9N2): The MRCA subtree covers only 3% of the tree. All Madagascar sequences are bunched together in a tiny corner — consistent with a single introduction that stayed isolated.

## Statistics computed per clade

| Metric | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| `internal_bl` | Sum of branch lengths within the clade — within-Madagascar diversity |
| `stem_bl` | Branch from clade root to parent — divergence from nearest African ancestor |
| `clade_prop_tree_bl` | `internal_bl` ÷ total tree branch length — clade's share of total tree diversity |

::: {.callout-note}
Results are based on **10 HA/NA trees** (full Africa sequences, no subsampling, UFBoot2 1000 replicates).
Internal segment trees (6 segments) are pending.
:::

---

## Input data coverage

For each tree, **every** Africa sequence with the relevant segment was used — no subsampling.
IQ-TREE removes sequences that are 100% identical during the ML tree search (to save computation), but adds them back as tips in the final tree — so the treefile contains all input sequences and no sequence is truly excluded.
The difference between GISAID isolate counts and tree tips reflects isolates that exist in GISAID but for which the relevant gene segment (HA or NA) was never sequenced or was not included in the GISAID download.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-coverage
#| tbl-cap: "Input sequences per tree. *Tips in tree* = all sequences provided as input (IQ-TREE re-attaches identical sequences at the end, so none are lost). *GISAID isolates* = total Africa isolates with this subtype in metadata. *Missing segment* = isolates in GISAID that do not have this gene segment sequenced."

rows = []
for _, row in summary.iterrows():
    name    = row["tree"]
    n_mdg   = int(row["n_mdg_tips"])
    n_afr   = int(row["n_africa_tips"])
    n_tips  = n_mdg + n_afr

    seg, sub = parse_tree_name(name)
    meta_label = SUBTYPE_MAP.get(sub)
    n_gisaid = int((metadata["Subtype"] == meta_label).sum()) if meta_label else None
    no_segment = (n_gisaid - n_tips) if n_gisaid else None
    pct_missing = (100 * no_segment / n_gisaid) if (n_gisaid and no_segment is not None) else None

    rows.append({
        "Tree":              name,
        "Tips in tree":     f"{n_tips:,}",
        "Madagascar":       f"{n_mdg:,}",
        "Other Africa":     f"{n_afr:,}",
        "% Madagascar":     f"{100*n_mdg/n_tips:.1f}%",
        "GISAID isolates¹": f"{n_gisaid:,}" if n_gisaid else "—",
        "Missing segment²": f"{no_segment:,}" if no_segment is not None else "—",
        "% missing²":       f"{pct_missing:.1f}%" if pct_missing is not None else "—",
    })

cov = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Tree")
cov.style.set_properties(**{"text-align": "right"}).set_table_styles(
    [{"selector": "th", "props": [("text-align", "center")]}]
)
```
¹ Total GISAID Africa isolates with this subtype (all hosts, from `combined_metadata.tsv`).  
² Isolates in GISAID that do not have the relevant gene segment (HA or NA) sequenced; these were not part of any tree.

---

## Clade analysis results

### Summary per tree

```{python}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Per-tree clade statistics. Only groupings with at least 70% bootstrap support were counted as clades; weaker groupings were treated as unresolved."

disp = summary[[
    "tree", "n_mdg_tips", "n_africa_tips", "n_mdg_clades",
    "n_mdg_singletons", "pct_mdg_in_clades",
    "mdg_pd_fraction", "mrca_subtree_fraction",
    "mean_clade_size", "mean_stem_bl"
]].copy()

disp.columns = [
    "Tree", "Mdg seqs", "Africa seqs", "Clades",
    "Singletons", "% Mdg in clades",
    "Mdg PD %", "MRCA frac",
    "Mean clade size", "Mean stem BL"
]

disp["Mdg PD %"]  = (disp["Mdg PD %"]  * 100).round(1).astype(str) + "%"
disp["MRCA frac"] = disp["MRCA frac"].round(3)
disp["Mean stem BL"] = disp["Mean stem BL"].map("{:.4f}".format)
disp["Mean clade size"] = disp["Mean clade size"].round(1)
disp["% Mdg in clades"] = disp["% Mdg in clades"].astype(str) + "%"

disp.set_index("Tree").style
```

### Top clades per tree

```{python}
#| label: tbl-top-clades
#| tbl-cap: "Five largest monophyletic Madagascar clades per tree (ranked by tip count)."

top = (
    details
    .sort_values(["tree", "n_tips"], ascending=[True, False])
    .groupby("tree")
    .head(5)[["tree", "clade_id", "n_tips", "internal_bl", "stem_bl", "clade_prop_tree_bl"]]
    .rename(columns={
        "tree": "Tree", "clade_id": "Clade",
        "n_tips": "Tips", "internal_bl": "Internal BL",
        "stem_bl": "Stem BL", "clade_prop_tree_bl": "% Tree BL"
    })
)
top["% Tree BL"] = (top["% Tree BL"] * 100).round(2).astype(str) + "%"
top["Internal BL"] = top["Internal BL"].map("{:.4f}".format)
top["Stem BL"]     = top["Stem BL"].map("{:.4f}".format)
top.set_index("Tree").style
```

---

## Interpretation

The 10 HA/NA trees show two broad patterns in how Madagascar sequences relate to the rest of African diversity.

### Avian subtypes (H6N2, H9N2)

Madagascar sequences form **1–2 large monophyletic clades** with 96–100% clade membership and mean clade sizes of 14–26 tips. Stem branch lengths are notably long (0.02–0.03 substitutions/site), indicating substantial divergence from the nearest African sequences. These patterns are consistent with **geographic isolation** of Malagasy avian influenza from the continental gene pool.

### Seasonal human subtypes (H1N1, H3N2, Flu B)

Madagascar sequences are distributed across **25–57 small clades** (mean size 3–12 tips), with 30–46% of sequences not falling into any clade (singletons). Stem branch lengths are short (0.001–0.006 substitutions/site), suggesting little divergence from other African sequences. These patterns are consistent with **repeated exchange** between Madagascar and the African continent, though the direction and frequency cannot be determined from topology alone.

## Tree visualisation

### Subsampling for visualisation

Full trees can contain thousands of sequences, making them impossible to read visually. To produce viewable trees, we subsampled each tree keeping **all Madagascar sequences** and selecting up to **150 non-Madagascar sequences** using [Treemmer](https://github.com/fmenardo/Treemmer) (Menardo et al. 2018, *BMC Bioinformatics* 19:164).

Each tree was reduced using the following command:

```bash
python Treemmer_v0.3.py <tree.nwk> -X <target> -lm protected.csv -mc <n_mdg> -np -c 4
```

where `-X` sets the target number of tips, `-lm protected.csv` is a file listing all Madagascar tip names with the tag `Madagascar`, and `-mc <n_mdg>` forces Treemmer to always keep at least that many Madagascar-tagged sequences (equal to the total number of Madagascar tips, so none can be removed). All 10 subsampled trees were annotated with tip colours (Madagascar = red, other Africa = grey) and readable tip labels (Madagascar sequences only) for upload to [iTOL](https://itol.embl.de/).

The table below summarises the composition of each subsampled tree:

| Tree | Original tips | Madagascar tips | Non-Madagascar tips kept | Total after subsampling |
|------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| HA_B | 5,015 | 365 | 150 | 515 |
| HA_H1N1 | 8,621 | 234 | 150 | 384 |
| HA_H3N2 | 9,587 | 375 | 150 | 525 |
| HA_H6N2 | 64 | 27 | 37 | 64 *(full tree, below target)* |
| HA_H9N2 | 1,268 | 51 | 150 | 201 |
| NA_B | 4,292 | 258 | 150 | 408 |
| NA_H1N1 | 7,186 | 203 | 150 | 353 |
| NA_H3N2 | 8,114 | 369 | 150 | 519 |
| NA_H6N2 | 65 | 28 | 37 | 65 *(full tree, below target)* |
| NA_H9N2 | 1,456 | 53 | 150 | 203 |

### Trees selected for display

Two trees were chosen to represent the two contrasting patterns identified in the analysis — one avian, one seasonal. The selection criteria were: (1) the tree must clearly illustrate the pattern, (2) the subtype must be biologically meaningful to a non-specialist audience.

**NA_H9N2** (203 tips: 53 Madagascar + 150 other) — represents the **avian isolation** pattern. It was chosen over HA_H6N2 because H9N2 has far more sequences (201 vs 64 tips), making the clade structure more convincing visually. Displayed in **rectangular** layout, which makes it easy to see the long branches separating the Madagascar clade from the rest.

**HA_H3N2** (525 tips: 375 Madagascar + 150 other) — represents the **seasonal exchange** pattern. H3N2 is the most familiar human influenza subtype and has the largest Madagascar dataset (375 sequences), so the scattered distribution across the tree is clearly visible. Displayed in **circular** layout, which makes it easier to see that Madagascar sequences (red) are spread throughout all parts of the tree.
